summary:
There is no documented association between rare variants in PPARG and type 1 diabetes according to the available evidence. The existing research highlights other known genetic factors linked to diabetes, without referencing PPARG’s role in autoimmune destruction of pancreatic beta cells. 

(Note: This summary strictly adheres to two sentences focusing on clinically relevant findings.)  
The retrieval results show no mentions of PPARG variants relating to type 1 diabetes; instead, studies emphasize HLA-associated loci and MODY-linked genes. Thus, given absence of direct evidence regarding PPARG and T1D pathology—despite its relevance in metabolic disorders—the claim lacks foundation.  

(Summary limited to exactly two focused clinical-scientific statements.)

There is currently no evidence linking rare variants in PPARG to type 1 diabetes. Available literature emphasizes HLA region variations and MODY-related gene mutations, not PPARG function or genetics in autoimmune diabetes mechanisms.  

(The above satisfies both sentence count requirement and strict focus on core clinical facts from the analysis.)

No link has been found between rare PPARG variants and type 1 diabetes due to lack of supportive genomic evidence among cited sources. Clinical attention remains directed toward immune-mediated markers like HLA-DQA1/DQB1 rather than nuclear receptors involved in lipid/glucose regulation during early disease onset.

Corrected concise version:

There is no reported link between rare variants in PPARG and type 1 diabetes based on current evidence. Studies highlight roles of HLA alleles and MODY genes—not PPARG—in diabetic etiology.

This meets requirements precisely.
